params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Favorite Fruits data. Apples=6, Bananas=4, Oranges=5, Grapes=3. Key: each fruit symbol = 1 vote.
Data table appears alongside graph, showing same Favorite Fruits values.

steps.text: You made your own graph. Nice. Now let's read some graphs other people made. Here's the thing about graphs... they're not just pictures. Every part is actually telling you something. Let's figure out what.
Click on the category that got the MOST votes.

validator.description: Student clicked Apples, correct, 6 votes

params.description: Apples row highlights to confirm selection

steps.text: Apples got the most. 6 people chose it. You read that right from the graph.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Apples row highlighted. Favorite Fruits data.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the symbols in each row. Which row has the most?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count each row: Apples has 6 symbols, Bananas has 4, Oranges has 5, Grapes has 3. Which number is biggest?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System highlights Apples row and demonstrates counting the symbols

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I count the symbols in each row: Apples has 6, Bananas has 4, Oranges has 5, Grapes has 3. The biggest number is 6. So Apples got the most votes.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph in reading mode. Favorite Fruits data. Apples=6, Bananas=4, Oranges=5, Grapes=3.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values alongside the graph.

steps.text: You found the most. Now try the opposite. Which fruit got the LEAST votes?
Which fruit got the LEAST votes?

validator.description: Student clicked Grapes, correct, 3 votes

params.description: Grapes row highlights to confirm selection

steps.text: Grapes got the least, just 3 votes. You compared the rows and found the smallest one.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Grapes row highlighted. Favorite Fruits data.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Look at the numbers next to each row. Which one is smallest?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count each row: Apples 6, Bananas 4, Oranges 5, Grapes 3. Which row has the fewest symbols?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Grapes row highlights as system demonstrates the answer

steps.text: I'll walk us through this. Count each row: Apples has 6, Bananas has 4, Oranges has 5, and Grapes has only 3. The smallest number is 3, so Grapes got the least votes. Click Grapes.

Vertical picture graph appears. Animals at the Zoo data. Lions=5, Monkeys=7, Elephants=3, Penguins=6. Key: each animal icon = 1 animal.
Data table appears showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: Every part of a graph has a job. Watch.

params.description: Graph title highlights

steps.text: The title tells us what the data is about. Animals at the Zoo.

params.description: Category labels highlight

steps.text: The labels tell us what each column shows.

params.description: Key highlights

steps.text: The key shows the scale. Here, each animal picture equals 1 animal. That's a scale of 1. Now you try. Click on the part that tells us what each symbol means.
Click on the part that tells us what each symbol means.

validator.description: Student clicked the key, correct

steps.text: That's the key. It shows the scale. Each symbol equals 1 animal.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Animals at the Zoo data.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong component on first attempt

steps.text: Look at the bottom of the graph. The key shows what each picture stands for.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong component on second attempt

steps.text: Here's a hint: The key is at the bottom. It tells us that each animal picture equals 1 animal.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong component three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Key highlights as system demonstrates

steps.text: Let me show you. The key is this part at the bottom. It tells us what each symbol means. Here it shows that each animal picture equals 1 animal. Click the key.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Animals at the Zoo data. Key highlighted.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: Here's a different picture graph. This one goes up and down instead of side to side. We call that vertical. Same idea as before, though. Use the key to read it.
How many monkeys are at the zoo?

validator.description: Student answered 7, correct

steps.text: 7 monkeys. You counted 7 symbols, and each one equals 1.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Animals at the Zoo data.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

validator.description: Student selected 5: counted Lions instead of Monkeys

steps.text: Let's think about this together. We need to count the Monkeys column, not Lions. Look at the label at the bottom to find Monkeys.

validator.description: Student selected 6: counted Penguins instead of Monkeys

steps.text: Here's a hint: Check the labels at the bottom. We're counting Monkeys, not Penguins. Find the Monkeys column and count up.

validator.description: Student selected 8: miscounted or added extra value

steps.text: You're working on it. Count just the monkey symbols in that column, starting from the bottom. Each symbol equals 1 monkey.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights and counts each monkey symbol from bottom to top: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

steps.text: Let's work through this step by step. First, find the Monkeys column at the bottom. Then count each symbol going up: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. Seven symbols means 7 monkeys.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Animals at the Zoo data.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: Let's read one more value from this graph. Which animal has fewer than 4 at the zoo?
Which animal has FEWER than 4 at the zoo? Click on it.

validator.description: Student clicked Elephants, correct, 3 animals

params.description: Elephants column highlights to confirm selection

steps.text: Elephants. There are only 3, and that's fewer than 4.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Look at the number next to each column. Which one is less than 4?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

steps.text: Here's a hint: Count the symbols in each column. Fewer than 4 means 3, 2, 1, or 0. Elephants has 3.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Elephants column highlights to show the answer

steps.text: Let me walk us through this. Fewer than 4 means we need a number less than 4. Lions has 5, too many. Monkeys has 7, too many. Elephants has 3, that's fewer than 4. Penguins has 6, too many. Elephants is the answer.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Animals at the Zoo data.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: You're reading picture graphs. Now let's look at a different way to show the same kind of data: bar graphs.

params.description: Picture graph transforms into bar graph with vertical axis and tick marks at 0 to 8 showing same data
Vertical bar graph appears. Animals at the Zoo data. Axis 0 to 8 by 1s, tick marks at each whole number.
Picture graph removed after transformation

steps.text: Same information, different look. Instead of counting symbols one by one, we just read where the top of the bar lines up.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Animals at the Zoo data. Axis 0 to 8 by 1s.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: In a bar graph, the HEIGHT of the bar tells us the amount. Look at the Monkeys bar. How many monkeys does the bar show? Click on the Monkeys bar and find where the bar ends.
Click on the bar for Monkeys and find where the bar ends.

validator.description: Student clicked Monkeys bar, correct

params.description: Horizontal line draws from top of Monkeys bar to vertical axis, 7 highlights

steps.text: Look at the Monkeys bar. Find where the top of that bar lines up with the numbers.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

steps.text: You figured out which had the most and which had the fewest. Now let's try some different kinds of questions.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Favorite Colors data.

steps.text: How many MORE students chose Blue than Yellow?
This is a comparison question. When we ask 'how many more,' we're comparing two amounts. We find the difference by subtracting.
Blue has 8. Yellow has 6. Since more students chose Blue, we subtract: Blue minus Yellow. So, how many more students chose Blue than Yellow?
How many more students chose Blue than Yellow?

validator.description: Student answered 2, correct

steps.text: 2 more students. You found the difference: 8 minus 6 equals 2.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Blue and Yellow bars highlighted. Favorite Colors data.

validator.description: Student selected 8: identified Blue's total instead of the difference

steps.text: You found Blue's total. The question asks how many MORE students chose Blue than Yellow. We need to find the difference between 8 and 6.

validator.description: Student selected 14: added the two values instead of finding the difference

steps.text: Here's a hint: 'How many more' means we subtract, not add. Blue has 8, Yellow has 6. The difference tells us how many more.

validator.description: Student selected 3: subtraction error or misread the bar values

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count the Blue bar carefully: it goes to 8. Count the Yellow bar: it goes to 6. Now subtract: 8 minus 6.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Visual shows the difference between Blue bar (8) and Yellow bar (6), highlighting the 2-unit gap

steps.text: I'll walk us through this problem. Blue reaches 8 on the scale. Yellow reaches 6. When we ask 'how many more,' we subtract the smaller number from the bigger number: 8 minus 6 equals 2. So 2 more students chose Blue than Yellow.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Blue and Yellow bars highlighted. Favorite Colors data.

steps.text: New question: How many FEWER students chose Red than Blue?
How many fewer is also a comparison. We're still finding the difference.
How many fewer students chose Red than Blue?

validator.description: Student answered 3, correct

steps.text: 3 fewer students. Blue has 8, Red has 5. The difference is 3.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Red and Blue bars highlighted. Favorite Colors data.

validator.description: Student selected 5, the value of the Red bar instead of the difference

steps.text: Let's think about this together. You found how many chose Red. But the question asks how many FEWER, which means we need to find the difference between the two bars.

validator.description: Student selected 8, the value of the Blue bar instead of the difference

steps.text: Here's a hint: You found how many chose Blue. But we need the difference. How many fewer means subtract Red from Blue.

validator.description: Student selected 13, the sum of Red and Blue instead of the difference

steps.text: You're working on it. Here's what helps: How many fewer means we subtract, not add. We need to find how far apart the two bars are.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Visual highlights the gap between Red bar (5) and Blue bar (8), showing 3 units difference

steps.text: I'll walk us through this. Blue has 8 students. Red has 5 students. To find how many fewer, I subtract: 8 minus 5 equals 3. Red has 3 fewer students than Blue.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Red and Blue bars highlighted. Favorite Colors data.

steps.text: Here's a different kind of question. How many students chose Yellow OR Red IN ALL?
In all means we want the TOTAL. We add to find totals.
How many students chose Yellow or Red in all?

validator.description: Student answered 11, correct

steps.text: 11 students in all. Yellow has 6, Red has 5. 6 plus 5 equals 11.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Yellow and Red bars highlighted. Favorite Colors data.

validator.description: Student selected 6, which is only Yellow's value, not the total

steps.text: You're working on it. That's just Yellow. The question asks for Yellow OR Red in all. We need to add both numbers together.

validator.description: Student selected 5, which is only Red's value, not the total

steps.text: Let's think about this together. That's just Red. But we need Yellow OR Red in all. Look at both bars and add.

validator.description: Student selected 1, possibly confusing the number of categories with the total count

steps.text: Here's a hint: in all means total. Look at how many students chose Yellow, then how many chose Red. Add those two numbers.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Yellow bar shows 6, Red bar shows 5, both values pulse to demonstrate addition

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Yellow has 6 students. Red has 5 students. In all means we add them: 6 plus 5 equals 11. So 11 students chose Yellow or Red in all.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Yellow and Red bars highlighted. Favorite Colors data.

steps.text: Some questions take two steps to answer. Let's work through one together.
Here's the question: How many MORE students chose Yellow or Red together compared to Green?
Step 1: Find how many chose Yellow or Red in all. How many students liked Yellow or Red in all?
How many liked Yellow or Red in all?

validator.description: Student answered 11, correct

steps.text: 11 in all. Yellow has 6, Red has 5. 6 plus 5 equals 11.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Green bar highlighted. Total 11 displayed. Favorite Colors data.

validator.description: Student selected 5, counted only Red

steps.text: You counted Red correctly. The question asks for Yellow OR Red together. Count both bars.

validator.description: Student selected 6, counted only Yellow

steps.text: You counted Yellow correctly. The question asks for Yellow OR Red together. Count both bars.

validator.description: Student selected 14, counted Yellow and Red and Blue

steps.text: Here's a hint: The question asks for Yellow OR Red only. Count just those two bars, not Blue.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Yellow and Red bars highlight, values combine to show 11

steps.text: I'll walk us through this. Yellow has 6 students. Red has 5 students. When we add them together, 6 plus 5 equals 11. That's how many liked Yellow or Red in all.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Yellow and Red bars highlighted. Favorite Colors data.

steps.text: Step 2: Now compare that total to Green.
How many MORE is 11 than Green's 4?

steps.text: 7 more students like Red or Yellow compared to Green. You used two steps: first you added to find a total, then you subtracted to compare.

steps.text: Here's what helps: When the question asks how many MORE, we subtract the smaller number from the bigger number. Try 11 minus 4.

Vertical picture graph appears. Pets We Have data. Dogs=7, Cats=9, Fish=4, Birds=6, Lizards=1. Key: each X = 1 pet.

steps.text: Look at this graph. It shows how many pets students in a class have. Here's the question we're about to answer: How many fewer students have fish than dogs? Before we solve it, which categories do we actually need to look at?
Which categories do you need? Select all that apply.

validator.description: Student selected Dogs and Fish, correct

steps.text: You picked exactly what you need: Dogs and Fish. The other categories? They don't matter for this question.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Pets We Have data.

validator.description: Branch 2: Student selected only one correct category (Dogs or Fish), but not both

steps.text: You're on the right track. The question asks about fish compared to dogs, so you need both categories to answer it.

validator.description: Branch 3: Student selected only incorrect categories (none of the required Dogs or Fish)

steps.text: Let's look at the question again: How many fewer students have fish than dogs? Which two categories does that question name?

validator.description: Branch 4: Student selected at least one correct category (Dogs or Fish) and at least one incorrect category

steps.text: You got Dogs or Fish, which is good. But the other pets you picked aren't part of the question. It only asks about fish and dogs.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Dogs and Fish columns highlight to show they are the only relevant categories

steps.text: Let me walk you through this. The question asks: How many fewer students have fish than dogs? That means we're comparing two categories: Fish and Dogs. Those are the only two we need. Cats, Birds, and Lizards aren't mentioned in the question, so we ignore them. Select Dogs and Fish.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Pets We Have data.

steps.text: Now solve it. How many fewer means we're comparing to find the difference. How many fewer students have fish than dogs?
How many fewer students have fish than dogs?

validator.description: Student answered 3, correct

steps.text: 3 fewer students. Dogs got 7 votes, Fish got 4. The difference is 3.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Dogs and Fish highlighted. Cats, Birds, Lizards dimmed. Pets We Have data.

validator.description: Student selected 4, the number of fish votes instead of the difference

steps.text: Let's think about this together. That's how many fish votes there are. But we need the difference between dogs and fish.

validator.description: Student selected 7, the number of dog votes instead of the difference

steps.text: Here's a hint: that's how many dog votes there are. How many fewer means we need to find the difference, not just one category's total.

validator.description: Student selected 11, the sum of dogs and fish instead of the difference

steps.text: You're working on it. That's the total if we add dogs and fish together. But how many fewer asks us to compare, to find the difference between them.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Visual shows Dogs column at 7, Fish column at 4, with the 3-symbol difference highlighted

steps.text: I'll walk us through this. Dogs got 7 votes. Fish got 4 votes. To find how many fewer, I compare: 7 minus 4 equals 3. The difference is 3 fewer students.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Dogs and Fish highlighted. Cats, Birds, Lizards dimmed. Pets We Have data.

steps.text: Here's the question: How many MORE students have Cats than Fish and Lizards combined?
First, let's figure out how many students have Fish OR Lizards at home.
How many have Fish or Lizards combined?

validator.description: Student answered 5, correct

steps.text: 5 total. 4 have Fish, 1 has Lizards. 4 plus 1 equals 5.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Cats column highlighted. Total 5 displayed. Pets We Have data.

validator.description: Student selected 3: counted only Fish, ignoring Lizards

steps.text: Let's think about this together. We need BOTH Fish and Lizards. Count the Fish symbols, then count the Lizards symbols, then add them.

validator.description: Student selected 4: counted only Fish

steps.text: You counted Fish correctly. The question asks for Fish OR Lizards combined. Look at the Lizards column too.

validator.description: Student selected 6: may have added Fish and Lizards but made a calculation error

steps.text: Here's a hint: Count the Fish symbols, then count the Lizards symbols. Add those two numbers together carefully.

validator.description: Student selected 13: added all categories except Cats

steps.text: You're working on it. We only need Fish and Lizards, not all the categories. Look at just those two columns.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights Fish column showing 4 symbols, then Lizards column showing 1 symbol, then displays 4 + 1 = 5

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Fish has 4 symbols. Lizards has 1 symbol. When we add them, 4 plus 1 equals 5. So 5 students have Fish or Lizards combined.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Pets We Have data.

steps.text: Now, how many MORE students have Cats compared to Fish and Lizards together?
How many MORE have Cats than the total of Fish and Lizards?

validator.description: Student answered 4, correct

steps.text: Right. 4 more students have Cats than Fish or Lizards. You broke that down into two steps and worked through both.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Cats column highlighted. Total 5 displayed. Pets We Have data.

validator.description: Student selected 5: gave the total of Fish and Lizards instead of the difference between Cats and that total

steps.text: Let's think about this a bit more. 5 is how many have Fish or Lizards. But the question asks how many MORE have Cats. We need to compare: 9 Cats minus 5 Fish or Lizards.

validator.description: Student selected 9: gave the Cats total instead of the difference

steps.text: Here's a hint: 9 is the total for Cats. The question asks how many MORE. That means we subtract: 9 Cats minus the 5 students who have Fish or Lizards.

validator.description: Student selected 14: added Cats and the combined Fish/Lizards instead of subtracting

steps.text: You're getting there. The word MORE tells us to compare by subtracting, not adding. We need 9 minus 5, not 9 plus 5.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights Cats column (9 symbols), then Fish and Lizards combined (5 symbols), then shows 9 - 5 = 4

steps.text: I'll walk us through this problem. Cats has 9 students. Fish and Lizards combined has 5 students. To find how many MORE have Cats, we subtract: 9 minus 5 equals 4. So 4 more students have Cats.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Cats column highlighted. Total 5 displayed. Pets We Have data.

steps.text: You can read picture graphs and bar graphs. You know that how many more and how many fewer mean subtract to find the difference. And in all means add to find the total. Let's see how you use what you learned.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Pets We Have data.
